definition: Japan's debt sustainability rests on a unique and non-replicable configuration: NIIP +70% GDP, >90% domestic JGB ownership, BoJ >50% ownership, persistent current account surplus, and deflationary backdrop keeping r<g. The US fails every one of these conditions and should not use Japan as evidence that high debt is indefinitely sustainable.
evidence: Deflationary backdrop enabling low rates for 27 years
US and China fail every condition
Japan corporate credit patterns are generalizable even where sovereign dynamics are not
US has negative NIIP vs Japan's +70% GDP
US ~30% foreign ownership vs Japan >90% domestic
No sustained US deflation vs Japan's deflationary backdrop
US twin deficits vs Japan's persistent CA surplus
Japan's political economy sequence generalizes even when financial outcomes don't
KB uses Japan asymmetrically — cautionary for debt, dismissed for positive outcomes
open_questions: Is Japan the central case (default prior for advanced economies) or the boundary condition?
Are Japan's structural features load-bearing or coincidental?
What happens to Japan's sustainability as BoJ exits YCC and demographics worsen further?
How should Japan's recent outperformance (rising wages, rates, corporate profitability, equity outperformance) modify the demographic doom consensus?
Is Japan's corporate credit generalizability conditional on similar monetary policy regimes?
Does Japan's political economy sequence (denial → accommodation → monetization → coordination) generalize?
If Japan sustains >2% inflation, >3% wages, and equity outperformance for 5+ years, does consensus require fundamental revision?
Is asymmetric usage of Japan as analogue a correctable bias?
